HIST 11203 (1312): Hist Of Civilization II  
**World History from 1500**  
University of Arkansas at Little Rock

*Dates:*  May 26 \- June 29, 2026  
*SUMMER\_FIRST\_5\_WEEKS\_2026*   
*HIST 11203-991  // Hist Of Civilization II*  
*Office Hours:* [by appointment](https://calendly.com/nemarvin/office-hours)  
*Instructor:* Nathan Elliot Marvin, Ph.D.  
*Email Address:* [nemarvin@ualr.ed](mailto:nemarvin@ualr.edu)u

# **Overview**

This course explores the processes that shaped global history from 1500 to the turn of the twenty-first century. We’ll work together to interpret primary and secondary sources and develop evidence-based arguments about the past and our ever-changing world.

# 

# **Themes**

Everything is shaped by what came before. Thinking historically means attending to how and why things change over time, and in this course, we’ll take that approach as we seek to understand the historical processes that shaped our contemporary world. In particular, we’ll explore the processes by which once-distant regions have become entangled by empire, migration, commerce, and the circulation of ideas. To do so, we’ll examine primary sources, including first-hand recollections, institutional pronouncements, films, and maps. The goals of this course are ambitious; no single framework can adequately account for such a broad sweep of human history. I welcome your constructive feedback.

# 

# **Goals**

This class is part of the revised UALR Core curriculum, which includes several Core Competencies/Learning Outcomes:

1. Written Communication: Write clearly and effectively using language appropriate to the discipline of history.  
2. Oral Communication: Present ideas and arguments clearly and effectively using language appropriate to the discipline of history.  
3. Knowledge/Critical Thinking: Analyze connections between historical events and interactions among different groups and societies.  
4. Technology: Use technology to locate and evaluate relevant primary & secondary historical sources.  
5. Ethical Reasoning: Recognize diverse perspectives when analyzing historical events and contexts.

# 

# **Accessibility**

My personal goal is for all features of this course to be accessible to all students. Alt text is provided for images; videos include captions; PDFs are OCR-enabled and legible by screen readers. If you identify a barrier to accessibility for any item, please email me. I will do my best to correct the issue.

# **Regular and Substantive Interaction**

This asynchronous online course includes regular and substantive interaction between instructor and students throughout the term. I provide weekly announcements, instructor-created guidance materials, and regular feedback on assignments. I will be  actively present in Perusall discussions and monitor student progress in Blackboard (checking once per two days). If a student appears to be falling behind, I will initiate contact. Students are encouraged to reach out at any time via Blackboard Messages or email.

# 

# **Grading & Blackboard**

Blackboard is the Learning Management System with which our university has contracted for online course delivery. Blackboard Ultra (Bb) will be used to post readings, messages, and assignments. Be sure to check the syllabus (this document) and calendar feature in Blackboard to stay on top of due dates. If you have problems submitting assignments, contact Bb support and CC me, so I am aware of the problem. To review instructor feedback on written assignments, navigate to “My Grades.” Be advised that Bb automatically collects statistics on how frequently users access features. I periodically review these stats. If I notice you are falling behind on deadlines, I will be in touch with you to make sure everything is alright. 

You may keep up with your progress in the course—including individual grades and assignment feedback—in the “Gradebook” on Blackboard. Your overall grade appears as a percentage and is based on a running average of all graded items. Items not yet due or not yet graded are not included, so your overall grade may change significantly after a missed assignment or a particularly strong score.

Your final grade will follow the scale below and will be based on the following categories:

|  Letter Percentage\* A 90–100 B 80–89 C 70–79 D 60–69 F 59 and below \*rounded to the nearest integer  |  19% ⇒ Quizzes: open-book, multiple-choice quizzes, including introductory quizzes (3%) and unit content quizzes based on lecture material (16%) 30% ⇒ Discussion/Perusall: collaborative annotation of primary sources (30%)  51% ⇒ Exhibit Assignments: digital exhibits focused on research and primary source analysis (51%) (Exhibit 1 \= 24%; Exhibit 2 \= 27%)    |
| :---- | :---- |

## **![][image1]**

## **Quizzes & “Knowledge Checks”**

You’ll take regular quizzes throughout the semester to help you keep up with weekly materials and check your understanding of key lessons. You have two attempts for each quiz, and the higher score will count. **Course content quizzes cover lecture content–not the textbook content.** Most quizzes include an extra credit question on the theme “Power and Place.”

Please note: the unit review quizzes will NOT cover the lecture content. However, you will find “Knowledge Checks” at the end of each textbook reading. These are short review questions—one per reading—designed to connect the textbook and lecture content and reinforce the “Big C’s of Historical Thinking” from Week 1\. You have unlimited attempts and can retry until you arrive at the correct answer. These are *not* required, nor are they graded. They are designed to help you keep up with the textbook reading, which is key to contextualizing our primary source materials. 

## **Discussion (Perusall)**

In this course, we will use the online tool “Perusall” to read and annotate primary sources as a class. Follow the guides on Blackboard to sign up for your free account with Perusall. Perusall is a space where you can develop and perfect the principal skills associated with this course: how to think historically, approach source materials critically and productively, interpret and debate the meaning of historical sources, and marshal evidence from primary and secondary sources to support a persuasive historical argument.  Perusall serves as a venue to ask for clarification about a term or passage and share your thoughts with your peers. Your grade in this category will depend on the frequency of your participation, your level of engagement with peers, and the quality of your comments and questions. Perusall does not yet have a functioning mobile app, so you must use a desktop computer, tablet, or laptop to complete these activities. You should become familiar with the “screenshot” tools on your devices (on Macs, this is Command+Shift+4; PCs usually have a separate button on the keyboard or use the application “Windows Snipping”). Missed assignments in this category automatically earn the lowest possible grade for the assignment: 50%. 

## 

## **Exhibit Assignments**

We will create two interactive digital exhibits: one based on a pictorial map from the David Rumsey Map Collection and one based on a set of photographs from the Allison WWII Photographs Collection. This project is designed to demonstrate our mastery of primary source analysis and historical thinking skills. We should select sources that interest us, contextualize them historically (considering when, where, and why they were produced), and conduct additional research to help explain key details in our sources to an audience of our peers. We will then build a digital exhibit presenting our interpretation, which must include at least 250 words of written content, and create an accompanying audio or video recording (1.5 to 3 minutes) that walks through our process and analysis. Our exhibit will be shared with classmates for peer review, and we will be expected to provide thoughtful, constructive feedback on others’ work. After receiving feedback from peers and the instructor, we will revise our exhibit accordingly.

**Score:** Exhibit 1 (24%) and Exhibit 2 (27%). *If you earn a perfect grade or are otherwise satisfied with your grade on Exhibit 1, you may email the professor to request exemption from Exhibit 2, in which case your Exhibit 1 grade will be applied in place of Exhibit 2\.*

## 

# **Extra Credit**

## **Office Hours Meeting**

Meet with me; boost your grade\! If your grade happens to fall below a “C” (i.e., \<70) over the course of the semester, signing up for one of my office hours times or scheduling a separate appointment to meet with me via phone or video call will result in one grade from either the Perusall/Discussion or Quiz categories changed to “100%.” Limit one grade boost per semester ;)

# **Schedule**

*Subject to change at the discretion of the instructor.*

| Due Date | Assignment(s) Due | Location on Blackboard | Topics/Folders Covered  |
| :---: | ----- | :---: | ----- |
| **Tuesday,May 26** | Course Overview Quiz Plagiarism Quiz Historical Thinking Basics Quiz (*together: 3% of overall grade*) | Start Here/ Course Orientation (check folders) | Start Here / Course Orientation |
| **Wednesday,May 27** | “Virtual Meet and Greet” (Perusall) “Meeting of Cortés and Moctezuma” (Perusall) (*together: 5% of overall grade*) | Discussion / Perusall |  |
| **Thursday,May 28** | Quiz (Unit 1): Early Modern Empires *(4% of overall grade*) | Unit 1 | Introductions / First Encounters; European Empires in the Americas I, II; Eurasian Empires; Islamic Empires; Global Trade Networks |
| **Tuesday,June 02** | “Quapaw Three Villages Robe” (Perusall) “Three Perspectives from Women in the Colonial Americas” (Perusall) (*together: 5% of overall grade*) | Discussion / Perusall |  |
| **Wednesday,June 03** | Quiz (Unit 2): Age of Revolutions *(4% of overall grade*) | Unit 2 | Atlantic Slave Trade; Age of Revolutions I, II, III; Industrial Revolutions I, II |
| **Thursday,June 04** | “Mapping the Age of Exploration” (Perusall) “The Life of Omar Ibn Said” (Perusall) (*together: 5% of overall grade*) | Discussion / Perusall |  |
| **Tuesday,June 09** | “Freedom Demands in the Age of Revolution” (Perusall) “Imagining a Monument” (Perusall)  (*together: 5% of overall grade*) | Discussion / Perusall |  |
| **Wednesday,June 10** | \[No submission due: exhibit prep\] |  |  |
| **Thursday,June 11** | Map Exhibit *(24% of overall grade*) | Map Exhibit | (Comprehensive: Historical Thinking Basics & Primary Source Analysis) |
| **Tuesday,June 16** | Quiz (Unit 3): Modern Empires *(4% of overall grade*) | Unit 3 | Industrial Revolution Goes Global; Modern European Empires I, II; Modernization in Asia; Nationalism and Revolution in China |
| **Wednesday,June 17** | “Intellectual Responses to Industrialization” (Perusall) “Sun Yat-sen & Qiu Jin” (Perusall) (*together: 5% of overall grade*) | Discussion / Perusall |  |
| **Thursday,June 18** | Quiz (Unit 4): 20th Century *(4% of overall grade*) | Unit 4 | WWI; Rise of Dictators; WWII & the Holocaust; Decolonization; Cold War; World at the Turn of the 21st Century |
| **Tuesday,June 23** | “Personal Stories from the Holocaust Era” (Perusall) “Battle of Algiers” (Perusall)  (*together: 5% of overall grade*) | Discussion / Perusall |  |
| **Wednesday,June 24** | \[No submission due: exhibit prep\] |  |  |
| **Thursday,June 25** | Photo Exhibit *(27% of overall grade*) | Photo Exhibit | (Comprehensive: Historical Thinking Basics & Primary Source Analysis) |
| **Tuesday,June 30** | Final Day of the Course / Last Day to Submit Missing Work (No late work accepted after this date; final grades due shortly thereafter.) |  |  |
| **Wednesday,July 01** | \[12:00 p.m. — Deadline for instructor to submit final grades to the Office of Student Records\] |  |  |

# **Textbooks**

The readings for this course are a combination of extracts from the following:

- Strayer, Robert, and Eric W. Nelson. *Ways of the World: A Brief Global History with Sources.* 3rd OR 4th Edition. Boston: Bedford/St. Martin's, 2016 (hereafter, Strayer & Nelson)  
- Kordas, Ann, Ryan J. Lynch, and Brooke Nelson. *World History, Volume 2 From 1400 Volume 2\.* OpenStax, 2022 (hereafter, Kordas, Lynch & Nelson).

I include all required readings on Blackboard. There is no need to purchase unless you prefer reading a hard copy. 

# 

# **Technology** 

To succeed in this course, you will need access to a reliable Internet connection. Perusall, in particular, does not yet have a functioning app, so you will also need access to a personal computer or one in a computer lab. You will also need access to a word processor (e.g., GoogleDocs, MS Word, TextEditor, Pages). If you do not have a computer, contact the CARE Team: [http://bit.ly/CTself-referral](http://bit.ly/CTself-referral). The Care Team and Ottenheimer Library may be able to provide laptops that can be borrowed at no cost for the semester. If, during the course of the semester, you lose access to either a computer or a reliable internet connection, please contact me immediately so that I can connect you with resources and so that we can discuss options for moving forward. Email [studentsuccess@ualr.edu](mailto:studentsuccess@ualr.edu) with any technology access-related questions you might have. Additional information is available at [https://ualr.edu/studentsuccess](https://ualr.edu/studentsuccess).

# 

# **Communication**

The best way to reach me is through my UALR email account ([nemarvin@ualr.edu](mailto:nemarvin@ualr.edu)). I check and respond to email several times a day during weekdays (i.e., Monday-Friday, 9-5). If I have not responded to email within 24 hours during the week, please try again. You may also send your message using the Blackboard Messaging function (preferred). In either case, please introduce yourself and please be specific about your particular question or concern so I can best assist you. 

Office hours are held virtually (Zoom or in-person) by appointment. Use the following link to sign up for a spot: [https://calendly.com/nemarvin/office-hours](https://calendly.com/nemarvin/office-hours). If you do not see available hours, send me an email to set up a meeting. 

## 

# **Attendance**

Because this is an asynchronous online course, attendance is measured through regular participation in academically related activities, including submitting assignments, completing quizzes, and contributing to Perusall discussions. Logging into Blackboard alone does not constitute attendance.

If a student does not access the course site or communicate with the instructor for seven consecutive days, the instructor may initiate a non-attendance administrative withdrawal through the Office of Records/Registrar in accordance with university policy.

# 

# **Late Work**

As a general rule, I am uncompromising on quality, but I can be accommodating on time. I know you all have busy lives and a lot going on, so be in touch with me in advance (at least 24 hours) if you anticipate needing more time on a deadline. Otherwise, I expect all work to be completed and turned in by the date on which it is due. You may submit assignments whose deadlines have passed. Assignments submitted later than the deadline will be accepted but may be docked by 10% of the total possible points for the assignment. Please let me know via email when you submit late work. 

# 

# **Academic Integrity**

## **Plagiarism**

When you use others’ words or ideas without giving them credit, you are plagiarizing. All sources, including information gathered on the Internet, must be correctly cited in all assignments. Anyone who is involved in cheating or plagiarism will automatically receive the lowest possible grade for the assignment and may be referred to the Integrity and Grievance Committee for disciplinary action. 

## 

## **Generative AI**

It is cheating to submit texts rendered fully or in part by another person or by software, including generative AI (artificial intelligence) tools such as OpenAI’s ChatGPT. Submissions that are composed entirely using AI with no (or virtually no) input from you will usually not meet the expectations of an assignment. AI may appear to be a helpful assistant, but it is not a compelling author. Trust your own interpretive voice, and communicate with me if you are struggling, rather than deferring to AI. 

#

Find more information here: [https://ualr.edu/chasse/student-resources/](https://ualr.edu/chasse/student-resources/).
